title: Approaches to costing: full, contribution
summary: 9609 AS — full (absorption) costing vs contribution (marginal) costing, when to use each, and contribution per unit.
learningObjectives: Calculate contribution per unit and total contribution.
Compare appropriate uses of each costing method.

simpleExplanation.title: Two ways to allocate costs
simpleExplanation.summary: Full costing spreads all production overheads into the unit cost. Contribution costing only deducts variable costs from selling price — the remainder contributes to fixed costs and profit.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Full costing is like splitting the entire household bill equally among flatmates regardless of who used what — fair for long-term sharing. Contribution costing is like each person paying only for what they consumed tonight — better for deciding whether to order another takeaway right now.
steps: Full (absorption) cost = variable cost + share of fixed overheads per unit.
Contribution per unit = selling price − variable cost per unit.
Use contribution for short-run decisions (break-even, special orders).
Use full cost for long-run pricing and financial reporting.

flashcards.back: All production costs, including a share of fixed overheads, are allocated to each unit of output.
Only variable costs are deducted from selling price; the remainder is contribution toward fixed costs and profit.
Selling price − Variable cost per unit.
Short-run decisions: break-even, accept/reject special orders, make-or-buy, limiting factors.
Long-run pricing, inventory valuation, published accounts, full-cost-plus pricing.
Total contribution = (SP − VC) × units sold, or total revenue − total variable costs.

flashcards.front: Risk of using full cost for a one-off order?
flashcards.back: May reject profitable orders if price covers variable costs and contribution but not full allocated overhead.

faq.q: Why does break-even use contribution costing?
faq.a: Break-even asks how many units cover fixed costs. Only contribution (not full unit cost) goes toward fixed costs each period. Full costing mixes fixed overheads into unit cost and gives the wrong break-even output.

faq.q: Can inventory valuation differ between methods?
faq.a: Yes. Under absorption costing, closing inventory includes a share of fixed overheads; under marginal costing it does not. This can change reported profit when inventory levels change.

sections.content: **Full vs contribution costing** (5.4.2) is essential before **break-even (5.4.4)**. Cambridge questions often test whether you know **which method applies** — not just the formula. Contribution for short-run; full cost for long-run and accounts.

Under **full costing**, each unit produced absorbs a **fair share of fixed production overheads** (rent, factory supervision, depreciation) as well as direct materials and direct labour.

**Full unit cost = Direct materials + Direct labour + Share of overheads**

Used for **inventory valuation**, **published financial statements**, and **long-run pricing** where all costs must be recovered over time.

Under **contribution costing**, only **variable costs** are charged to each unit. Fixed costs are treated as a **period cost** — deducted in total from contribution, not allocated per unit.

**Contribution per unit = Selling price − Variable cost per unit**

**Profit = Total contribution − Fixed costs**

Contribution per unit = SP − VC

Total contribution = (SP − VC) × quantity

Profit = Total contribution − Fixed costs

items: **Special order:** Accept if price > variable cost and contributes to fixed costs (unless capacity is full and better use exists).
**Limiting factor:** Rank products by **contribution per unit of scarce resource** (e.g. per kg of material, per machine hour).
**Do not** use full unit cost to reject an order that still earns positive contribution.

sections.question:
A firm makes one product. Selling price $40, variable cost $22, fixed costs $36 000 per month, output 3 000 units.

(a) Calculate contribution per unit.
(b) Calculate total contribution and profit.
sections.solution:
(a) Contribution per unit = $40 − $22 = **$18**

(b) Total contribution = $18 × 3 000 = **$54 000**
Profit = $54 000 − $36 000 = **$18 000**

sections.question: A customer offers a one-off order of 500 units at $28 each. Normal SP is $40; VC is $22; the firm has spare capacity. Full unit cost (with overheads) is $35. Should the firm accept?
sections.solution:
**Use contribution costing** (short-run, spare capacity).

Contribution per unit on order = $28 − $22 = **$6**
Total extra contribution = 500 × $6 = **$3 000**

Fixed costs unchanged in short run → **accept** the order.

Rejecting because $28 < full cost ($35) would be **wrong** — the order still covers variable costs and adds $3 000 toward fixed costs and profit.

sections.content: In **recommend** questions, state the costing approach first: "Using contribution costing because this is a short-run decision…" then show the calculation.
